home.heroDescription: ReviewRadar scores every pull request for complexity, surfaces what needs your attention, and shows what's blocked — across every repo you track. Whether you're an IC looking for your next review or a lead tracking team health, everything you need is in one place. All in your browser. No backend.
home.feature1Desc: Auto-refresh on a configurable interval. Your PR list stays current without lifting a finger. Browser notifications for approvals, comments, and build changes.
home.feature2Desc: Filter by all, mine, needs attention, approved, changes requested, pending, or conflicts. Click any label or status badge in the table to filter by that value instantly.
home.feature3Desc: Review status, build state, mentions, labels, merge conflicts, and last updated — all visible without leaving the dashboard.
home.feature4Desc: PRs that need your eyes float to the top. Mentions, stale reviews, and failed builds are all factored in. Sort by any column.
home.feature5Desc: Your GitHub token is encrypted server-side and stored in a secure cookie — never in localStorage. All API calls are proxied through Cloudflare Functions so your token never touches the browser's network tab.
home.feature6Desc: Sweep across as many repositories as you have access to. One dashboard, all your PRs. Or focus on a single repo when you need to.
home.feature7Desc: Every PR scored 0–100 using a file-type-weighted algorithm. Instantly spot the monsters hiding in your queue — before they become merge conflicts.
home.feature8Desc: Drag, drop, and show or hide any column. Size, complexity, files, approvals — your dashboard, your layout. Persisted across sessions.
home.feature9Desc: Built for global teams. Switch between English and French instantly. The entire UI — including the guide and complexity docs — adapts seamlessly.
home.case1Title: The monster PR hiding in plain sight
home.case1Desc: A 200-file refactor lurks among ten small bugfixes. Reviewers waste time on safe PRs while the dangerous one sits unreviewed. GitHub shows them all as equal lines in a list. ReviewRadar's complexity scoring weights every file by type and churn. That refactor scores 94 — bright red, impossible to miss. The bugfix scores 12 — green, quick to approve.
home.case2Desc: An engineer opens GitHub and scrolls through thirty PRs. Half are their own. Five they've already reviewed. Three are drafts. They burn ten minutes just figuring out what needs their eyes. The Needs Attention filter instantly surfaces only PRs that are not yours, not drafts, not yet reviewed by you, and have no approvals. Zero noise.
home.case3Desc: A build fails in repo A. A merge conflict sits in repo B. Changes are requested on repo C. Your team discovers each blocker hours after it happened — usually when someone asks 'is this ready to ship?' The Blocked filter aggregates every blocker across every repo in one view. The Reports page breaks down status, author load, and complexity spread so you see team health at a glance.
home.step1Title: Create a GitHub Personal Access Token
home.step1Description: Go to <githubLink>GitHub Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens</githubLink>. Create a token with <codeRepo>repo</codeRepo> scope for private repos or <codePublicRepo>public_repo</codePublicRepo> for public only.
home.step2Description: Paste your token into the dashboard. It's stored locally in your browser via localStorage and never sent to any server. The app calls GitHub's API directly from your browser.
home.step3Description: Load all your PRs or specify a repository. Enable auto-refresh and browser notifications to stay up to date. Filter, sort, and sweep across your code review queue.
home.ctaDescription: No account. No install. Just your GitHub PAT and a browser tab.
home.securityFeature1Desc: Tokens are encrypted with AES-GCM using a secret key that never leaves Cloudflare's edge. The encrypted cookie is HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite-Strict.
home.securityFeature2Desc: PR descriptions and comments are sanitized with DOMPurify before rendering. A strict Content Security Policy blocks inline scripts and unauthorized resource loading.
home.securityFeature3Desc: Tokens never touch localStorage or the browser's network tab. Even if an attacker injects JavaScript, they cannot steal your GitHub token.

dashboard.title: ReviewRadar
dashboard.subtitle: Live PR Dashboard
dashboard.pleaseEnterPat: Please enter a GitHub PAT in settings

guide.title: Getting Started Guide
guide.step1Title: 1. Create a GitHub Personal Access Token
guide.step1Description: ReviewRadar uses the GitHub API to fetch your pull request data. To get started, you need a Personal Access Token (PAT):
guide.step1List1: Go to <githubLink>GitHub Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens</githubLink>
guide.step1List3: For classic tokens, select the repo scope (and read:org if you need private org repos).
guide.step1List4: For fine-grained tokens, select the repositories you want and grant Pull requests read access.
guide.step1List5: Copy the generated token and paste it into ReviewRadar when prompted.
guide.step2Description: Once your token is saved, add the repositories you want to track:
guide.step2List1: Open the Settings panel in the app.
guide.step2List3: Click Add — the repo will appear in your tracked list.
guide.step2List4: You can add or remove repositories at any time.
guide.step3Description: The dashboard gives you a real-time overview of all open pull requests across your tracked repositories:
guide.step3List1: Sort and filter PRs by status, author, label, and more.
guide.step3List2: See build status, review status, and how long each PR has been open.
guide.step3List3: Click any PR title to open it directly on GitHub.
guide.step3List4: Use the refresh button to pull the latest data on demand.
guide.step4Description: ReviewRadar analyses every PR's changed files to surface two useful metrics:
guide.step4List1: Size — shows the raw diff footprint (+additions / −deletions) and file count, so you can spot oversized PRs at a glance.
guide.step4List2: Complexity — a 0–100 score that weights churn by file type. Generated files, images, and lockfiles are down-weighted or ignored, while source code and tests count fully. The score also factors in file spread and rewrite intensity.
guide.step4List3: You can show or hide either column from Settings → Table Columns.
guide.step5Description: The complexity score is computed in five explicit steps. No magic — just weighted churn, file count, and rewrite intensity combined with a logarithmic curve.
guide.step5Formula: score = ln(1 + weightedChurn) × 5.5 + ln(1 + files) × 10 + 5 × intensity
guide.step5Step1Desc: Every changed file is assigned a weight based on its path and extension. Generated, binary, and lock files are ignored.
guide.step5Step1W1: Weight 1.0 — source code, tests, runtime templates
guide.step5Step1W2: Weight 0.5 — config, infra, CI, JSON, Dockerfiles
guide.step5Step1W4: Weight 0.0 — generated dirs, images, lockfiles, minified bundles
guide.step5Step1W5: Per-file churn is capped at 500 lines
guide.step5Step2Desc: For each relevant file, churn = max(additions, deletions) × weight. All files are summed to get total weightedChurn. A PR with 5 files of 800 lines each scores higher than 30 files of 1 line each.
guide.step5Step3Desc: ln(1 + files) × 10 rewards (or penalises) context-switching across many files. The logarithm means going from 1→2 files is a bigger jump than 49→50.
guide.step5Step4Desc: intensity = churnRatio ÷ (1 + churnRatio), where churnRatio = totalChanges ÷ (1 + |additions − deletions|). Pure rewrites (add ≈ del) score ~5. Pure net additions score ~2.5.
guide.step5Step5Desc: The three components are added together. The result is naturally capped at 0–100 by the logarithmic curve.
guide.step5LabelDesc: The final label is the more severe of the file-count tier and the score tier. A 75-file PR with a low score is still Enormous.
guide.step6Description: The dashboard groups pull requests into status categories to help you prioritise. Here's exactly what each one means.
guide.step6NeedsAttentionDesc: A PR needs your attention when ALL of the following are true:
guide.step6NeedsAttention1: It is NOT authored by you
guide.step6NeedsAttention3: You have NOT reviewed it yet
guide.step6BlockedDesc: A PR is blocked when ANY of the following are true:
guide.step6Blocked2: The build or checks are failing
guide.step6Blocked3: There is a merge conflict (needs rebase)
guide.step6Overlap: These categories can overlap — a PR can simultaneously need your attention AND be blocked. The stats bar counts each independently.
guide.step7Description: ReviewRadar supports two ways to authenticate with GitHub:
guide.step7OAuthDesc: The easiest way to get started. Click the 'Login with GitHub' button on Settings or any dashboard page. You'll be redirected to GitHub to approve the app, then back to ReviewRadar automatically.
guide.step7OAuthList1: Click 'Login with GitHub' on Settings or any page where you see the option.
guide.step7OAuthList2: Approve the app on GitHub. The requested scope is repo (for private repos) and read:user.
guide.step7OAuthList3: You'll be redirected back to ReviewRadar automatically. Your token is now active for 30 days.
guide.step7OAuthNote: If your organization has OAuth App restrictions, you may get 404 errors on org repos. Use a PAT instead, or ask your org admin to approve the ReviewRadar OAuth App.
guide.step7PatDesc: A fine-grained or classic PAT gives you full control over permissions. PATs bypass org OAuth restrictions and work everywhere.
guide.step7PatList1: Go to <githubLink>GitHub Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens</githubLink>.
guide.step7PatList2: Generate a token with the repo scope (or public_repo for public repos only).
guide.step7PatList3: Paste the token into ReviewRadar Settings and click Save.
guide.step8Description: ReviewRadar is built with security as a first-class concern. Here's how your data is protected:
guide.step8List1: Encrypted cookies — Your GitHub token is encrypted with AES-GCM using a server-side secret (SESSION_SECRET). The ciphertext is stored in an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie.
guide.step8List2: No client-side token exposure — The browser never sees your raw token after submission. All GitHub API calls go through a Cloudflare Functions proxy.
guide.step8List3: XSS protection — PR descriptions and comments are sanitized with DOMPurify before injection. A strict Content-Security-Policy blocks unauthorized scripts.
guide.step8List4: Rate limiting — The API proxy enforces per-IP rate limits to prevent abuse.
guide.step8List5: Path validation — Only whitelisted GitHub API endpoints can be proxied. Arbitrary URLs are rejected.
guide.step9Description: Common issues and how to resolve them:
guide.step9List1: 404 on organization repos with OAuth — Your org likely has OAuth App access restrictions. Switch to a PAT, or ask your org admin to approve the ReviewRadar OAuth App.
guide.step9List2: Rate limit exceeded — The proxy allows 1000 requests per minute per IP. If you hit this, wait a moment and retry. GitHub's own limit is 5000/hour.
guide.step9List3: No PRs showing — Check that you have repositories selected on the dashboard. Also verify your token has the repo scope for private repositories.
guide.step9List4: Invalid state on OAuth login — This usually means the OAuth cookie expired before you approved on GitHub. Try logging in again. If it persists, clear cookies for the site.
